22. Method of Types

When the distribution is specifically over a finite alphabet , we can use the a framework called the method of types, which leads to Sanov’s theorem, both more and less general that Cramer’s theorem.

Let and logarithms in base-2.

22.1. Type and Type Classes

Definition 22.1. Let be a sequence drawn from finite . The empirical distribution, or type, of is a probability distribution over the same alphabet defined as follows:

(22.1)

The type can be viewed as an estimate of the tue distribution that generated the sequence . Since , it follows from WLLN that .

(22.2)

The set of types is the set of all possible types for sequences of length generated from an alphabet . It is depicted as blue points on the shaded simplex and increasingly dense as increases.

The set of sequences of legnth whose type is equal to is and is called the type class.

22.2. Properties of Types

Lemma 22.1. The number of types is polynomial in the sequence length:

(22.4)

Proof

Each count is .

(more precisely use stars and bars).

□

The number of types is subexponential but the number of sequences is exponential (). Hence at least one type class must be exponential large. In fact we will show that essentially all the type classes are exponentially large.

22.3. Large Deviation Analysis via Types (Sanov’s Theorem)

We care about events that deviate through types not through the order of the symbols. Any event of interest can be expressed in the form

(22.8)

Use the following abuse of notation:

If samples are genertaed by then the probability of any type class decays exponentially according to the KL divegence. Hence the exponential that corresponds to the tpye closer to dominates the sum. This is the idea of Sanov’s theorem

22.4. Example: Large Deviations of Smple Averages

The I-projection of onto the boundary lies along an exponential family wit choice of representing the intersection. As we saw before the closest type minimizes the exponential.

22.5. Example: Universal Source Coding

Exlporing in ps8#2. Instead of encoding the entire sequence, encode the type class and the index within that type class (e.g. lexicographic)

nice because switching from sequence to type class flips the exponent to in terms of count.

22.6. Dominant Atypical Behavior

Sanov’s theorem suggests that when the event happens the type will be . With a bit more analysis we can verify that with high probability the observed type will be close to .

Theorem 22.3 (Conditional Limit Theorem).

Lemma 22.4 (Pinsker's Inequality).

This implies that convergence in divergence is stronger than convergence of the sequence.
